items.body: The RBC Canadian Open heads back to TPC Toronto at Osprey Valley for the 115th playing of Canada's national championship and the last full-field tune-up before the U.S. Open. Ryan Fox defends after a four-hole playoff win over Sam Burns last year, while Tommy Fleetwood and Matt Fitzpatrick lead the betting board in a field the tournament calls the strongest of the FedExCup era. With Scottie Scheffler and Rory McIlroy resting, stars like Aaron Rai, Brooks Koepka, and Collin Morikawa headline a loaded week where Aon Swing 5 points are still on the line and the renovated North Course should reward precise iron play into firm, runoff-heavy greens.

items.label: Tommy Fleetwood (+1150 to +1400):
items.body: The slight betting favorite after nearly grabbing the Memorial lead Sunday, with top-10 consistency and elite bogey avoidance suited to a par-70 that punishes loose approaches.

items.label: Matt Fitzpatrick (+1175 to +1400):
items.body: World No. 4 and co-favorite whose accuracy and iron play fit a Doug Carrick layout renovated specifically to test approaches into bunkered, undulating greens.

items.label: Sam Burns (+1325 to +1600):
items.body: The 2025 playoff runner-up with a score to settle at Osprey Valley, where his closing 62 and five straight birdies on the back nine nearly stole the title.

items.label: Collin Morikawa (+2200 to +2500):
items.body: A top-10 world player making his first Canadian Open start since 2019, with the ball-striking precision this course was built to reward.

items.label: Wyndham Clark (+2200 to +2500):
items.body: Fresh off a solo third at the Memorial and climbing the Aon Next 10 standings, with the power to attack the reachable par 5s that decided last year's playoff.

items.label: Brooks Koepka (+2500 to +2800):
items.body: Rested after skipping Memorial and hunting a Sunday tune-up before the U.S. Open, with six top-20 finishes already in his return season on Tour.

items.label: Robert MacIntyre (+2500 to +2800):
items.body: The 2024 champion and a proven winner in Canada who thrives when firm Ontario conditions demand controlled ball flight and spin.

items.label: Kristoffer Reitan (+2600 to +3000):
items.body: A Memorial top-six finisher now atop the Aon Next 10, with the aggressive iron play to capitalize if he keeps the driver in the wide fairways.

items.label: Aaron Rai (+4000):
items.body: The reigning PGA Championship winner back in action after a Memorial top-20, with the all-around game to contend if his putter heats up on a sub-20-under track.

items.label: Ryan Fox (+4000 to +4500):
items.body: The defending champion chasing back-to-back wins at TPC Toronto, with two playoff victories in 2025 proving he thrives when the week gets tight.

items.label: Venue:
items.body: TPC Toronto at Osprey Valley (North Course) in Caledon, Ontario, northwest of Toronto.

items.label: Established:
items.body: 1904; the RBC Canadian Open is Canada's national championship and the third-oldest event on the PGA Tour schedule.

items.label: Defending Champion:
items.body: Ryan Fox of New Zealand won in 2025 at 18 under, beating Sam Burns in a four-hole playoff on the par-5 18th with a clutch 3-wood to 7 feet.

items.label: Tradition:
items.body: Part of the Open Qualifying Series for The Open Championship, with a deep Canadian qualifying pathway and six past champions in the 2026 field including Nick Taylor and Robert MacIntyre.

items.label: Course:
items.body: TPC Toronto at Osprey Valley (North Course), Caledon, Ontario.

items.label: Dates:
items.body: June 11 through June 14, 2026.

items.label: Purse:
items.body: $9.8 million.

items.label: Format:
items.body: 147-player field, 72-hole stroke play; top 65 and ties make the 36-hole cut.

items.label: Course Profile:
items.body: A 7,389-yard par-70 retooled in 2023-24 with wide fairways, four demanding par 4s over 500 yards, runoff-heavy greens, and a 576-yard closing par 5 that produced 18 under and a playoff in 2025.

items.label: Coverage:
items.body: Thursday and Friday on Golf Channel (3-6 p.m. ET); weekend rounds on Golf Channel (1-3 p.m. ET) and CBS (3-6 p.m. ET Saturday and Sunday). PGA TOUR LIVE on ESPN+ (7 a.m.-6 p.m. Thu-Fri; 7:45 a.m.-6 p.m. Sat-Sun) and PGA TOUR Radio on SiriusXM.

items.label: Event Window:
items.body: Competition runs June 11 through June 14, 2026.
